content.problemStatement:
Here's a simple exercise that involves implementing a stack. 

In this exercise, you'll implement a basic stack to reverse a string using the stack's LIFO property.

TaskUpdate the functions `push()` and `pop()` within the class stack to output the reverses string.
constraints: S consists of printable ASCII characters (including spaces)
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single string S.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the characters of S in reverse order, preserving all characters exactly as they appear.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Each string is pushed onto a stack character-by-character and then popped, producing the reversed strings shown.
First string "OpenAI2023!" becomes "!3202IAnepO" after reversal. Second string is a palindrome, so the output equals the input.
Spaces are treated like any other character; reversing preserves the two leading and two trailing spaces.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by implementing a stack using arrays. We push each character of the string onto the stack and then pop each character off the stack to reverse the string. This approach works because a stack follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, which means the last character pushed onto the stack will be the first one to be popped off.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string. The total time complexity is O(T * N), where T is the number of test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the stack, where N is the length of the string.

code.Python3:
import sys
lines = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
if not lines:
    sys.exit()
T = int(lines[0])
out = []
for i in range(1, T + 1):
    s = lines[i]
    stack = list(s)
    rev = ''.join(reversed(stack))
    out.append(rev)
sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
